title: Fitting skirting board: coped internal corners
subtitle: First length square, second length coped to fit
excerpt: How to fit skirting board around a room with clean internal corners using the cope-and-butt method: running the first length into the corner square, cutting the face profile of the second length with a coping saw, and fitting it over the first.
sourceNotes: Standard UK joinery practice from Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery and the City & Guilds Site Carpentry Level 2 syllabus. Cope-and-butt joinery unchanged from Victorian trade practice.

content.text: A mitred internal corner on skirting looks clean when it is first fitted but opens as the timber moves with changes in humidity. A coped joint stays tight because one piece of skirting butts into the corner square and the second piece is cut to fit over the face of the first. As the timber moves, the cope simply rides the face; there is no open gap.
The cope-and-butt principle

The first skirting length in a room runs full width into the corner square, with its end cut square and butted tightly against the wall. The second length that meets it is cut at an internal 45-degree mitre across its full face profile, then the profile is cut along the mitre line with the coping saw. The resulting cut matches the face of the first piece exactly.
Fixing the first length
Step 1, measure and cut the first length.

Measure the first wall from corner to corner. Cut the board to length with square ends. On a solid wall, fix with screws into wall plugs at 600 mm centres; on a stud wall, locate the studs and nail or screw into them. Drive the fixings slightly below the skirting face so they can be filled. The first length must sit tight to the floor and flat against the wall.
Cutting the cope
Step 2, cut the 45-degree mitre.

Cut the end of the second skirting length at an internal 45-degree mitre so the face of the profile is revealed as a clean line. The mitre angle means the face is proud and the back is recessed. This face line is what the coping saw will follow.
Step 3, cut the cope with the coping saw.

Hold the coping saw perpendicular to the face of the board and cut along the mitre profile line, following the curved sections of the moulding profile. Keep the blade cutting on the waste side of the line. The cut must run at 90 degrees to the face so the resulting profile fits flat against the first piece.
Step 4, test and refine the cope.

Offer the coped end up against the fixed first length and check the fit. The profile should close tightly with no gaps. A small rasp, file, or coarse sandpaper around a shaped block can refine any tight spots.
Fixing the second length
Step 5, cut the second length to overall length.

Measure the wall from the cope end to the far corner and cut the far end to the required angle: square for a butt end, or mitred for an external corner. Fix the second length to the wall in the same way as the first.
Step 6, fill and paint.
Fill the screw/nail holes and any small gaps between the skirting top and the wall with paintable filler. Allow to cure and sand flush. Apply the finish paint.
Common mistakes
Cope joint with a visible gap at the face.
The coping saw blade wandered off the mitre line. Re-cut with the saw held consistently at 90 degrees to the face, and refine with a chisel or file.
Skirting not tight to the floor.
The floor is not level. Scribe the bottom of the skirting to follow the floor by running a compass or block of wood along the floor while marking the skirting, then cut along the line with a jigsaw.
